verdict.headline: A mature, uniformly strong TypeScript dice-library monorepo with no critical or high-severity findings — remaining work is observability "last-mile" and polish.
verdict.framing: Every remediation item is Medium or Low; the codebase is ready to build on, not to fix.

scorecard.headline: Well-maintained and highly modular (zero dead code per `knip`); dragged only by diverged `dice-ui` web/native component pairs and a 157-line codegen emitter function. (Report grade: B+)
~1.21:1 test-to-code ratio across 193 files with property, 9999-iter stress, and conformance layers; thin app-layer e2e is the only real gap. (Report grade: A−)

No hardcoded secrets, ReDoS-resistant notation parser with hard bounds, zero-dependency core, clean `bun audit`; minor gaps are a missing commit-time secret scanner and a `Math.random` RNG boundary note. (Report grade: A−)
Excellent error hierarchy and a deliberately hardened bot, but the Sentry tracker is a no-op stub and there is no liveness/SLO/alert for the single-instance worker.
Pure-compute engine with every unbounded loop (explode/reroll/unique/repeat) explicitly capped and enforced bundle budgets; two roller entry points sit near their size ceiling. (Report grade: A−)
Mechanically enforced stable-core DAG (`dependency-cruiser` clean: 0 cycles, 0 cross-game imports), genuinely zero-dep core, 19 MADR ADRs.
Elite lead time; Changesets + npm OIDC Trusted Publishing, SHA-pinned path-filtered CI with bench/SCA gates; soft spots are stale branches and single-maintainer bus factor.
Top-decile doc estate (root + per-package CLAUDE.md, 19 ADRs, two doc sites, a real ops runbook); drift risks are a stale bot README and unpublished TypeDoc.
100% config-as-code across Render/Netlify×2/EAS+npm with full toolchain pinning, SHA-pinned actions, and zero committed secrets (OIDC + `sync:false`); minor drift-detection gaps. (Report grade: A−)

narrative.executive_summary:
`randsum` is a single Bun-workspace TypeScript monorepo (~53.6k LOC across the zero-dependency `@randsum/roller` engine, the code-generated `@randsum/games` packages, the `@randsum/dice-ui` React-Native library, and five apps — CLI, Discord bot, two Astro doc sites, and an Expo playground). It publishes a dice ecosystem to npm under `@randsum`.

The headline finding is that there is **no headline problem**. Across nine quality dimensions the scorecard is uniformly strong — seven A-band, two B-band — with **zero Critical or High-severity findings**. Architectural invariants are mechanically enforced rather than merely documented (`dependency-cruiser` `arch:check` ran clean: 275 modules, 0 cycles, 0 cross-package violations); the untrusted-notation parser is ReDoS-resistant with hard input bounds; the test estate is genuinely deep (193 files, ~2,700 roller tests with property-based + 9999-iteration stress + RFC-2119 conformance layers); delivery is Elite-tier (Changesets + npm OIDC Trusted Publishing, SHA-pinned CI with bench/SCA gates); and the documentation estate is top-decile for the project's size (19 MADR ADRs, two doc sites, a real ops runbook).

The two B-grade dimensions — maintainability and reliability — are dragged by a small number of contained issues rather than systemic weakness: the `@randsum/dice-ui` web/native component pairs have diverged badly (the `QuickReferenceGrid` `.tsx`/`.native.tsx` differ by ~1,459 lines), and the single long-running service (`apps/discord-bot`) has its observability "last mile" unfinished — the Sentry error tracker is a no-op stub and there is no liveness signal, SLO, or alert for the single-instance gateway worker.

**Highest-leverage recommendation:** finish the bot's observability last mile (activate the error tracker, add a liveness check + one symptom-based alert + a minimal SLO, and unit-test the failure-path utilities). This is one bounded effort that lifts the bot's reliability posture from B to A and closes the only cluster of findings sourced by more than one dimension.
narrative.methodology:
This audit is **LLM-driven (v1)** — Claude reads code, manifests, configs, git history, and supplied artifacts and reasons about quality dimensions without running a full static-analysis toolchain. Findings are hypothesis-grade and should be confirmed with measurement before significant remediation. Where tools *were* run to ground a conclusion (`dependency-cruiser`, `knip`, `bun audit`, `size-limit`, and a clean `bun run build` + test pass), the report says so.

All nine dimensions ran; none were skipped. The **Infrastructure** dimension ran and is graded on managed-PaaS posture only — the repo has no IaC, containers, or self-managed orchestration, so those sub-checks are genuinely N/A (not failures). Several infrastructure and delivery conclusions are "configured but unverified" — derived from declared config (`render.yaml`, `netlify.toml`, branch-protection assumptions) rather than confirmed live state — and are flagged as such.

One cross-report discrepancy was reconciled against source: the performance report's draft claim that "no benchmark regression gate exists" was **incorrect** — `.github/workflows/ci.yml` does run a `bench (regression gate)` job (`fail-on-alert: true`, 150% threshold), matching the delivery report. The performance report has been corrected and that recommendation dropped as already-satisfied.

Anchored frameworks: ISO/IEC 25010, ISO/IEC 5055, OWASP ASVS / SAMM, DORA / Accelerate, Robert C. Martin package metrics, *Your Code as a Crime Scene*.

inventory.layout_notes: Single-repo (Bun workspace monorepo — one git repo, many sub-projects)

repos.name: randsum-monorepo
repos.description: Bun workspace monorepo for a dice-rolling ecosystem targeting tabletop RPGs. Core is `@randsum/roller`, a zero-dependency dice engine with built-in notation parsing/validation; game packages wrap it with game-specific interpretation; apps consume it (CLI, Discord bot, docs sites, Expo playground). Published to npm under `@randsum`.
repos.loc_breakdown: ~53,600 across source + tests (roller ~26.2k, games ~11.8k, dice-ui ~4.0k, site ~3.0k, discord-bot ~3.0k, expo ~2.7k, rdn ~2.2k, cli ~0.5k, scripts ~0.2k). Includes 193 test files.
repos.notes: Workspace structure — publishable packages: `@randsum/roller` (2.0.0, zero-dep core), `@randsum/games` (3.0.1, depends on roller), `@randsum/dice-ui` (2.0.0, RN component lib), `@randsum/cli` (2.0.0). Private apps: `@randsum/discord-bot` (1.1.2, Render worker), `@randsum/expo` (1.0.1, EAS native+web), `@randsum/site` (1.1.2, randsum.dev docs, Netlify), `@randsum/rdn` (1.0.0, notation.randsum.dev, Netlify). Game packages depend only on roller, never on each other. Codegen pipeline generates game packages from `.randsum.json` specs. Infrastructure: no IaC, no containers, no orchestrators; runtime config present (render.yaml, two netlify.toml, expo app.json/eas.json) — qualifies for the infrastructure dimension on managed-PaaS posture. 91 commits in the last 90 days; primary contributor Alex Jarvis (+ dependabot, Claude, github-actions).

source_artifacts.summary: Architecture, conventions, commands, build/publish/versioning rules for agents and contributors

dimensions.title: Maintainability
dimensions.summary: This is a well-maintained, highly modular TypeScript monorepo. The core `@randsum/roller` engine averages ~81 LOC/file with a one-file-per-modifier registry pattern that keeps the most-churned subsystem small and analysable; strict compiler flags plus an ESLint config banning `let`/`any`/`as unknown as` enforce consistency. Dead code is effectively nil — `knip` reports no unused files or exports (only three unused devDependencies). The two genuine maintainability drags are the React-Native UI library (`@randsum/dice-ui`), which carries seven `.tsx`/`.native.tsx` component pairs with massive divergence, and the games codegen emitter, whose 157-line `generateFunctionBody` is the single most complex function in the tree. Neither is critical. Report grade: B+.
framework_anchors: Tornhill, Your Code as a Crime Scene — churn × complexity

findings.title: `@randsum/dice-ui` web/native component duplication
findings.evidence: A line-level diff of the two `QuickReferenceGrid` variants shows ~1,459 differing lines — the web and native implementations have diverged so far they are effectively two parallel components sharing a name, not a thin platform shim over shared logic. `dice-ui` is the only package with files exceeding 500 LOC (two of the repo's three), and has the highest average file size (173 LOC/file vs. 81 for roller). Plus six other `.tsx`/`.native.tsx` pairs (NotationRoller, NumericStepper, RollResultPanel, RollSteps, TokenOverlayInput, useTheme).
findings.impact: Every behavioural change to a dice-UI component must be made twice and kept in sync by hand; divergence this wide means fixes silently land on only one platform. This is the repo's primary modifiability/duplication risk per ISO/IEC 25010 §6.5. The finding is the size and divergence, not the platform split itself (idiomatic React-Native).

findings.title: Oversized codegen function `generateFunctionBody`

locations.snippet: function spans ~157 lines; file is 405 LOC

findings.evidence: `generateFunctionBody` is ~157 lines — the single longest function found in the codebase — and sits alongside `generateMultiPoolBody` (~132 lines) and `generateRollParts` (~86 lines) in the same file. Max indentation depth ~5 levels. Per ISO/IEC 5055-MN-FN-LOC, functions over 50 LOC are flagged maintainability defects; this one is >3x the threshold.
findings.impact: This is string-emitting codegen (it builds the `.generated.ts` game files), so bugs here propagate to all eight game packages at once. Its length and nesting make the emitter the hardest part of the codegen pipeline to reason about and the riskiest to extend.

findings.title: Multi-pool notation parsing concentrates regex complexity

locations.snippet: 368 LOC, second-most-churned current file at 12 changes/2y

findings.evidence: The file hand-rolls multi-pool boundary detection with two interlocking `RegExp` constants (MULTI_POOL_BOUNDARY, FIRST_POOL_PATTERN) built from `String.raw` alternation lists, then walks `matchAll` results with index arithmetic and several defensive `undefined` guards. It is dense but well-commented and decomposed into named helpers (splitMultiPoolString, etc.).
findings.impact: This is inherent parsing complexity, not accidental — but it is both high-churn and the place where edge-case bugs in notation like `"1d20+2d6"` originate. It is the roller subsystem most worth additional property-test coverage rather than restructuring.

findings.title: High-churn roller barrels and shared type modules
findings.evidence: The top current-file churn is dominated by barrel re-export files and the shared `types/` directory — files that change whenever any export surface or type shifts. These are low-complexity (barrels are flat re-exports) so they are not hot-spots in Tornhill's churn×complexity sense, but the type cluster is a fan-in concentration point.
findings.impact: Low — high churn on low-complexity barrels is expected and healthy in a published-API monorepo. Flagged only to confirm the hot-spot analysis found no file that is both high-churn and high-complexity.

findings.title: Minor unused dependencies (dead-code hygiene)
findings.evidence: `bun run knip` reports 3 unused dependencies and 3 unused devDependencies, plus one unlisted binary (playwright). It reports zero unused source files and zero unused exports across the workspace. (knip emitted two non-fatal load errors for `apps/expo` configs referencing a missing `expo/tsconfig.base`, so expo's dead-export sweep is partial.)
findings.impact: Trivial — manifest hygiene only. The absence of any unused-export or orphan-file finding is a strong positive signal for ISO/IEC 5055 "unreachable code": this codebase has essentially no dead code surface.

metrics.notes: Highest in repo; web/native duplication is the driver

metrics.notes: Unused exports: 0 · Orphan files: 0 (knip) · Unused deps: 6 (manifest hygiene)

recommendations.title: Extract shared logic out of the `dice-ui` web/native component pairs into platform-agnostic hooks/utilities
recommendations.description: Especially the ~983/792-LOC `QuickReferenceGrid` variants — leave each `.native.tsx`/`.tsx` as a thin render shim. Reduces the two-places-to-change duplication that is the repo's top modifiability risk.

recommendations.title: Decompose `generateFunctionBody` (and siblings) in `emitBody.ts` into smaller named sub-emitters per concern
recommendations.description: Pool emission, modifier emission, multi-pool join. Lowers the blast radius of codegen changes that affect all eight game packages.

recommendations.title: Add targeted `fast-check` property tests around `parseArguments.ts` multi-pool splitting
recommendations.description: Signed/unsigned pool boundaries, special-die collisions like `D{..}`/`DD`/`d%`, to lock down the high-churn notation edge cases rather than restructuring the parser.

recommendations.title: Prune the six unused dependencies flagged by knip and fix the `apps/expo` `tsconfig.base` reference
recommendations.description: So knip can complete its expo dead-export sweep, restoring full dead-code coverage in CI.

dimensions.confidence: Directly observed: file sizes and counts, the >500-LOC file list, dice-ui web/native line-divergence (diff), per-package avg file size, churn counts, and dead-code results (knip). LLM-estimated: all cyclomatic-complexity buckets and function-length figures (treat "157 lines" as ±10%). Churn caveat: the repo was restructured, so raw 2-year churn is dominated by paths that no longer exist; hot-spot findings are computed against currently-existing files only, making hot-spot ranking directional. Generated code (2,756 LOC) excluded from complexity findings.

dimensions.title: Test Quality
dimensions.summary: The randsum monorepo has a mature, well-architected test suite. 193 test files span unit, property-based, stress, conformance-vector, integration, and e2e layers. The test-to-code ratio is ~1.21:1 (27,749 test LOC over 22,858 non-test source LOC), and the core engine alone runs 2,702 tests across 114 files. Test code is healthy: only one file exceeds 800 LOC, skips are few and each carries an explicit documented reason, and there are zero "flaky"/"TODO: fix" markers. Sampled tests show genuine depth (real invariants, a true cross-boundary integration test, dist-output smoke tests). Marks are withheld from a full A only because integration/e2e coverage is thin at the app layer and live coverage numbers live off-repo in Codecov. Grade A− (88/100).

findings.title: App-layer integration/e2e coverage is thin

locations.snippet: the one genuine un-mocked cross-boundary test

findings.evidence: The discord-bot's un-mocked cross-boundary integration test (real roller + seeded random → asserts the actual Discord embed) is exemplary but unique; the `cli` app has no equivalent and the docs-site builds have no smoke e2e. Pyramid shape is otherwise healthy and correctly weighted: a broad, deep unit + property base under the zero-dependency core, a deliberately thin integration/e2e cap.
findings.impact: This is the only marked gap holding test-quality below a full A. The right shape for a library-centric monorepo (the value is in the engine), but the app surfaces would benefit from replicating the discord-bot integration pattern.
