dimensions.confidence: High. Metrics are measured (file counts, LOC, skip/flaky greps, CI config inspection) rather than estimated. Coverage targets are cited from codecov.yml and CI; live coverage percentages are not measured here (no committed report) — Codecov hosts them. The 15 test failures observed on a raw worktree run were root-caused to a missing build artifact, not a defect, and confirmed green (17/0) after `bun run build`.

dimensions.title: Security
dimensions.summary: `randsum-monorepo` is, at its center, a pure-computation TypeScript library (`@randsum/roller`, zero runtime dependencies) plus thin consumers. This shape collapses most of the application-security attack surface: no database, no HTTP server in published packages, no user accounts, no session layer, no SQL. The dominant real risk for a dice library — untrusted-input parsing leading to DoS (ReDoS or unbounded compute) — is explicitly and competently mitigated with regex hygiene and hard numeric bounds. Security process maturity is high: committed SECURITY.md, `bun audit --audit-level=high` gated in pre-push and a scheduled workflow, an osv-scanner.toml documenting every accepted advisory, and all GitHub Actions pinned to full commit SHAs. No hardcoded secrets. Held just below A by a missing commit-time secret scanner and the `Math.random` default RNG note. Grade A− (89/100).

findings.title: No automated secret-scanning gate on commit
findings.evidence: Pattern grep and `bun audit` run, but a dedicated scanner (gitleaks / detect-secrets / GitHub push protection) is not wired into lefthook or CI. Pattern grep across all source/config returned zero suspected committed secrets; `.env` is gitignored; only `.env.example` placeholders are committed; Render declares Discord secrets `sync:false`.
findings.impact: The repo is clean today; adding the gate prevents future accidental commits and is cheap. Pure prevention.

findings.title: Default RNG is `Math.random` (non-cryptographic)
findings.evidence: The default RNG is `Math.random`. For an entertainment dice library this is fine and performant, and the API allows injecting a custom `randomFn`. But because the product's value proposition is dice fairness, it is worth a note that `Math.random` is not cryptographically secure and must never be relied on where unpredictability matters (any future money/wager or commit-reveal use).
findings.impact: Informational; not a vulnerability in the current entertainment context. Consumers needing CSPRNG-grade output should pass `{ randomFn }` backed by `crypto.getRandomValues`.

metrics.label: bun audit moderate / low (dev/build-time only, suppressed)
Dependency manifests with stale-major risk
metrics.notes: zero-dep core; discord.js on current major

metrics.label: License risk distribution
metrics.value: Permissive: all; Weak/Strong/Network copyleft: 0; Unknown: 0 (in published graph)

recommendations.title: Add a commit-time secret scanner
recommendations.description: Wire gitleaks or detect-secrets into lefthook.yml pre-commit (and/or enable GitHub push protection). The repo is clean today; this is pure prevention. (S-1, CWE-798.)

recommendations.title: Document the RNG security boundary
recommendations.description: Add a one-line note in `@randsum/roller` docs/README that the default `Math.random` is non-cryptographic and entertainment-grade, and show the `{ randomFn }` injection pattern. (S-2, CWE-338.)

recommendations.title: Run a real SCA + license tool as v2 confirmation
recommendations.description: This report is manifest-level hypothesis. Confirm with a full-tree scanner (Trivy/Snyk for CVEs; license-checker/ScanCode/FOSSA for licenses) across the Astro/Expo transitive trees where the suppressed advisories live.

recommendations.title: Re-evaluate the suppressed advisories on the next Astro/Expo major bump
recommendations.description: The suppressions in osv-scanner.toml are correct now but should expire, not become permanent.

recommendations.title: Consider a basic per-user/-guild rate limit on the Discord bot's compute commands
recommendations.description: The roller caps a single roll's work, but a flood of large-but-legal rolls could pressure the single worker instance. Low priority given the entertainment context and Discord's own interaction rate limits — a defense-in-depth note for the numInstances:1 worker.

dimensions.confidence: High for the core library and CI/secrets posture (static review covered the full parse/validate path, all package manifests, every GH workflow, both .env.example files, and a live bun audit run). Medium for the runtime apps (discord-bot, two Astro/Netlify sites, Expo) — reviewed statically; no running instance or deployed config inspected. Findings are hypothesis-grade; none are exploitation-confirmed.

dimensions.title: Reliability
dimensions.summary: This is a zero-runtime-dependency dice-engine monorepo: the publishable packages are pure computation with no network, DB, or queue I/O, so reliability there reduces to error-surface design and correctness — and on that axis the codebase is strong. A single coherent error hierarchy (all errors extend `RandsumError`) spans the ecosystem, `roll()` throws on invalid input, and there are no empty or all-swallowing catch blocks anywhere in non-test source. The only long-running surface, the `apps/discord-bot` Render worker, has been deliberately hardened (capped exponential backoff, structured JSON logger, in-process metrics, safety-net handlers, graceful shutdown, a pluggable error-tracker seam). The main residual gaps are an inactive error tracker (Sentry seam is a no-op stub), no health/liveness signal, no SLO/SLI or alerting, and no dedicated tests for the load-bearing reliability utilities.

findings.title: Error tracker is configured but never forwards (no-op Sentry stub)
findings.evidence: `captureException` logs a structured line and, when `SENTRY_DSN` is set, calls `forwardToSentry` — whose body is an empty no-op stub with a comment to swap in `Sentry.captureException`. `@sentry/node` is intentionally not a dependency (bundle-size tradeoff). So even with a DSN configured, exceptions are never delivered to an external tracker; they exist only in Render's log stream.
findings.impact: No aggregated error inventory, no deduplication, no alert-on-new-exception, no release-regression tracking. MTTR for a recurring bot exception depends entirely on manually grepping Render logs. The "error tracker" is presently observability theater unless the stub is wired up.

findings.title: No health / liveness signal for the single-instance gateway worker

locations.snippet: worker blueprint — no HTTP health check

findings.evidence: The bot is a Render `worker` (not a web service), so Render provides no HTTP health check, and the code exposes none. A "zombie" state — process alive but gateway silently disconnected and not reconnecting — would not be detected by the platform. There is no application-level heartbeat, shardDisconnect/shardReconnecting instrumentation, or watchdog.
findings.impact: A silent gateway stall is invisible until a user reports it. `numInstances` is correctly pinned to 1, which also means there is no redundancy to mask a stuck instance. Recovery is manual redeploy.

findings.title: No SLO / SLI definitions or alerting
findings.evidence: Metrics exist (per-command counters flushed every 5 min to a metrics.flush log line) and code comments anticipate "a future symptom-based alert," but no alert is defined and no target is documented. The RCA template references SEV1/2/3 but incidents/ is empty.
findings.impact: The team has the raw signal to define a symptom-based alert but has not closed the loop. Without an alert, the metrics flush is forensic-only. Appropriate severity for a hobby bot; would be High for a revenue service.

findings.title: Load-bearing reliability utilities have no dedicated tests

locations.snippet: no test references loginWithBackoff, metrics, errorTracker, logger, or interactionCreate error path

findings.evidence: loginWithBackoff.ts (retry/backoff loop with an explicitly injectable `sleep` seam), metrics.ts, logger.ts, and the interactionCreate catch/follow-up fallback are the components most likely to fail during an incident, yet none has a unit test. The injectable sleep/options signals test intent that was never realized.
findings.impact: The code paths that only execute under failure (backoff exhaustion, reply-after-error fallback, metrics flush on shutdown) are exactly the ones least exercised in normal operation, so regressions there ship silently.

findings.title: No distributed tracing or request/interaction correlation id
findings.evidence: Logs are structured JSON and captureException stamps interactionId/command/userId/guildId/phase onto error lines. But there is no trace-id spanning the full interaction lifecycle, no OpenTelemetry/tracing library, and routine logs carry no correlation id. For a single-service worker with no downstream calls this is the correct scope.
findings.impact: Minimal at current scale. Reconstructing a single interaction's full flow from logs requires the error path to have fired (only errors carry interactionId).

findings.title: `config.ts` throws at import time on missing env vars (fail-fast, with a restart-loop caveat)
findings.evidence: Missing DISCORD_TOKEN/DISCORD_CLIENT_ID throws a plain Error at module evaluation, before index.ts's initErrorTracker() runs. This is correct fail-fast, but a misconfigured (not missing) token surfaces only at client.login(), where loginWithBackoff (5 attempts, capped 30s) then process.exit(1) correctly avoids a tight crash-loop. The config-missing path throws before the tracker is initialized, so that specific failure is never captured by captureException.
findings.impact: Very low — a missing-env misconfiguration is a deploy-time error caught immediately on first boot. Noted as a minor asymmetry: the auth-failure path is hardened, the missing-config path is not.

metrics.label: Bare-except / silent-catch count
metrics.notes: grep for empty catch in non-test src returns none

metrics.label: Outbound calls missing timeouts
metrics.notes: Libraries make no I/O; bot's only outbound is discord.js

metrics.label: Metrics maturity
metrics.notes: In-process per-command counters flushed every 5 min; no histograms

recommendations.title: Activate the error tracker: wire `forwardToSentry` to a real transport
recommendations.description: Wire to Sentry.captureException (or a lighter HTTP-DSN POST to stay dependency-thin), gated on SENTRY_DSN. Closes the gap where the "error tracker" currently only logs.

recommendations.title: Add an application-level liveness signal for the bot
recommendations.description: Instrument discord.js shardDisconnect/shardReconnecting/shardError into the logger + metrics, and add a lightweight watchdog (or a tiny /healthz listener) so a stuck-but-alive gateway connection is detectable.

recommendations.title: Define one symptom-based alert from the existing metrics
recommendations.description: E.g. command error-rate over a 5-min window, or absence of any bot.ready/metrics.flush line, routed to a notification channel. The signal already exists; only the alert rule and routing are missing.

recommendations.title: Add unit tests for the load-bearing bot reliability utilities
recommendations.description: loginWithBackoff (exhaustion, cap, success-on-retry via the injectable sleep), metrics (flush/snapshot/timer lifecycle), logger (Error serialization, level→stream routing), and the interactionCreate error-reply fallback.

recommendations.title: Document a minimal SLO/SLI and error-budget one-liner for the bot
recommendations.description: E.g. "best-effort, single-instance; target: respond to >99% of interactions; budget-exhaustion policy: investigate before adding commands" so operational intent is captured.

recommendations.title: Capture the missing-config failure path through `captureException`
recommendations.description: Move initErrorTracker() ahead of (or restructure) the config.ts import-time throw, so all boot failures land in one place. Minor consistency improvement.

dimensions.confidence: Findings are directly observable from source, not LLM-inferred: the error hierarchy, the bot's reliability utilities, and render.yaml were read in full. The empty-catch count (0) and the absence of health endpoints/SLO files/tracing/alerting/observability libraries were verified by grep. The grade weighting reflects a judgment call: most of the codebase is pure-computation libraries where service-uptime checks are genuinely N/A, so the grade is anchored on error-surface design (excellent) and the single long-running service's operational posture (good-but-incomplete). Defect-signal analysis is code-only — runbooks/incidents/ is empty.

dimensions.title: Performance
dimensions.summary: This is a pure-compute TypeScript library ecosystem, not a service with a data tier. The performance-relevant surface is narrow and the team has clearly engineered against it: per-package bundle budgets are enforced in CI, the roll engine is fully synchronous with bounded loops everywhere, and a mitata benchmark suite exists with large-pool cases wired in. The two highest-leverage risk classes for a dice engine — unbounded explode/reroll/unique loops and bundle-size creep — are both actively guarded. The only material finding is tight bundle headroom on two roller entry points. Data-access findings (N+1, missing indexes, pagination) are N/A — there is no database or persistent data tier. Grade A− (90/100).

findings.title: Tight bundle headroom on two roller entry points

locations.snippet: 16 KB limit, measured 15.73 KB — 0.27 KB (1.7%) headroom
6.75 KB limit, measured 6.5 KB — 0.25 KB (3.7%) headroom

findings.evidence: `bun run size` (size-limit, fresh build) shows dist/index.js at 1.7% headroom and dist/tokenize.js at 3.7% headroom (brotli). Both will trip size-limit on the next non-trivial barrel addition or a behavior leak into the tokenize import graph. The gate already catches it.
findings.impact: A new modifier or notation primitive is likely to force a deliberate budget-vs-trim decision. The dist/tokenize.js isolation invariant (schema must not reference behavior symbols at module-init) should stay front-of-mind in review.

findings.title: Unbounded-loop risk is systematically bounded (positive finding)
findings.evidence: Every loop that could run forever has an explicit ceiling: reroll/unique (99 attempts), compound/penetrate (DEFAULT_EXPLOSION_DEPTH=1000), explodeSequence (length+1000), geometric (1000), repeat (MAX_REPEAT_COUNT=1000, MAX_REPEAT_DEPTH=10). Per-die accumulator complexity in reroll/unique was deliberately de-quadratic'd from O(n²) to a single linear pass, with large-pool benchmark cases exercising the paths. The benchmark regression gate (bench, fail-on-alert at 150%) exists in ci.yml.
findings.impact: Strong safety posture for a notation language that lets users write arbitrary explode/reroll combinations. Listed as a positive finding to record the audit's best evidence that the team treats hot-path complexity as a first-class concern.

metrics.label: Suspected N+1 patterns
metrics.value: N/A — no DB / ORM / data tier

metrics.label: Bundle budgets enforced
metrics.value: Yes — size-limit on roller (5 entry points) and games (7 bundles), brotli

recommendations.title: Benchmark regression gate exists; consider widening its trigger
recommendations.description: A bench (regression gate) job is already wired in ci.yml (github-action-benchmark, fail-on-alert: true, 150% threshold). The only residual item is that the gate triggers on roller path-changes — consider whether cross-package changes that affect roller hot paths should also trip it. (An earlier draft mis-stated this gate as absent; corrected post-review.)

recommendations.title: Watch the two tight bundle budgets
recommendations.description: dist/index.js (1.7% headroom) and dist/tokenize.js (3.7% headroom) will trip on the next non-trivial addition. The gate is already in size-limit, so this is "be aware," not "fix." Keep the dist/tokenize.js isolation invariant front-of-mind in review.

recommendations.title: Micro-optimize `applyAllModifiers` only if a benchmark demands it
recommendations.description: The per-step object rebuild + array spread in the reduce and the per-modifier [...rolls] snapshot are the only remaining non-linear-ish allocations on the hot path. With K ≤ 19 and typically 1-3 active modifiers, almost certainly not worth the readability cost — flagged for completeness.

dimensions.confidence: Medium-High. Findings combine static reading of the roller hot paths with one empirical measurement — bun run size (size-limit) was executed against a fresh @randsum/roller build, so the bundle numbers are real. The mitata benchmark harness was read but not run to completion; latency claims are structural inferences, not measured nanoseconds. None of the findings rise to "rework needed" — they are headroom and hygiene notes.

dimensions.title: Architecture
dimensions.summary: The RANDSUM monorepo exhibits a deliberate, well-governed layered architecture: a zero-dependency core (`@randsum/roller`) sits at the base, game packages and the component library depend only on it, and apps sit at the top — a strict DAG (roller <- {games, dice-ui} <- apps). The single most important architectural invariant — no cycles, no cross-game coupling, no reaching into internal src/dist — is not merely documented but mechanically enforced by a `dependency-cruiser` config wired into arch:check (pre-push and CI). A live run confirmed a clean graph (275 modules, 570 dependencies, zero violations). Decision history is unusually strong: 19 numbered ADRs, the most recent committed two days before the audit. The few observations below are minor refinements, not structural debt.

findings.title: Architecture invariants are enforced by tooling, not just convention (positive finding)
findings.evidence: Four forbidden rules encode the design contract: no-circular, no-cross-game-import-internal and -subpath (with an explicit allow for the shared @randsum/games/schema API), and no-internal-reach-in. dependency-cruiser 17.4.3 is pinned; the check runs in pre-push and the CI Gate. Live run during this audit: "no dependency violations found (275 modules, 570 dependencies cruised)."
findings.impact: The stable-core layering cannot silently erode. This is the difference between an aspirational architecture and a guaranteed one, and removes the primary risk this dimension usually flags (latent cycles).

findings.title: Exemplary, current ADR discipline (positive finding)
findings.evidence: 19 MADR-style ADRs with explicit Status sections, covering the unified games package, codegen, notation merge, literal vs branded types, modifier co-location, ESM-only output, API surface reduction, and tooling/app decisions. Most recent ADR committed 2026-06-21.
findings.impact: Decision rationale is discoverable and recent; the dependency-cruiser comments even back-reference architecture remediation IDs, showing the audit→decision→enforcement loop is closed.

findings.title: Stable-core layering matches actual imports (positive finding)
findings.evidence: roller declares no @randsum dependency (true zero-dep core). games and dice-ui depend on roller via workspace:~ only. Apps depend downward only. Roller's own internals are cleanly layered: roll/pipeline.ts composes lib/random, modifiers, types, and errors in a one-directional pipeline.
findings.impact: Confirms the documented layering is real, not just declared. The most-depended-on package (roller) is also the most stable (zero deps), satisfying SDP.

findings.title: Game packages are well-isolated bounded contexts (positive finding)
findings.evidence: Each game is generated from a declarative .randsum.json spec and emitted as an independent *.generated.ts entry. Grep for cross-game imports returned zero matches; the dependency-cruiser run confirms. The generated files call roll() directly.
findings.impact: Adding or changing a game cannot ripple into a sibling — the defining property of good bounded-context isolation.

findings.title: Coupling-magnet directories exist but are bounded and local
findings.evidence: roller's src/lib/ has ~12 intra-package importers; games' src/lib/ has ~9. These are real fan-in hubs, but each is package-local (no cross-package lib sharing), contents are cohesive and named by concern, and app-level utils/helpers directories are small and confined. The roller/src/lib/utils nesting and a generic lib/utils/constants.ts are the only mild grab-bag smells.
findings.impact: Low. These hubs are intentional and contained; the risk is only that lib/utils could accrete unrelated helpers over time. Worth periodic review, not remediation.

findings.title: CLI consumes roller via a bundled devDependency
findings.evidence: apps/cli imports roll from @randsum/roller/roll but lists roller under devDependencies, not dependencies. For a CLI that bundles its output (bunup) this is defensible — roller is inlined at build time. It does make the dependency edge invisible to consumers reading dependencies and to some tooling.
findings.impact: Minimal — purely a manifest-hygiene nuance. Confirm the published CLI bundle is self-contained (it is built with bunup).

metrics.label: Public API surface
metrics.notes: Subpath exports; ADR-009 documents surface reduction

recommendations.title: Keep the `lib/utils` and `lib/` hubs under periodic review
recommendations.description: So they do not accrete unrelated helpers; consider splitting roller/src/lib/utils by concern if it grows.

recommendations.title: Make the `apps/cli` → roller dependency edge explicit
recommendations.description: Optionally move @randsum/roller from devDependencies to dependencies in apps/cli/package.json (or add a comment documenting the bundled-edge rationale) so the edge is explicit to manifest-reading tooling.

recommendations.title: Maintain the ADR + `dependency-cruiser` discipline as the team grows
recommendations.description: It is the load-bearing control for this architecture and should be treated as a required gate, not optional.

dimensions.confidence: Cycle detection here is not the usual v1 heuristic: the repo ships dependency-cruiser 17.4.3 and arch:check was run live, so the no-cycles/no-cross-game-coupling/no-reach-in conclusions are directly observed. One caveat: the project sets tsPreCompilationDeps: false, so the cruiser ignores type-only edges (justified — erased at compile time); a pathological type-only cycle would not be caught, but none was found. Fan-in counts are grep-based approximations. The grade rests on exhaustively-verified structural invariants plus directly-counted ADRs, so confidence is high.

dimensions.title: Delivery Health
dimensions.summary: `randsum-monorepo` exhibits a mature, highly-automated delivery posture for a single-maintainer open-source monorepo. npm publishing is fully automated through Changesets + a workflow_run-gated Release workflow using npm OIDC Trusted Publishing (no long-lived tokens), and four deploy targets (Discord-bot → Render autoDeploy, two Astro sites → Netlify, Expo web/native → EAS) ship on push to main — effectively continuous deployment. Lead time is elite: merged PRs overwhelmingly close the same day. CI is unusually disciplined — SHA-pinned actions, path-filtered per-package jobs, an aggregating CI Gate, a benchmark regression gate, OSV/SCA scanning, dependabot auto-merge. The main soft spots are branch sprawl (16 unmerged remote branches, several ~3 months stale) and unverifiable branch-protection enforcement from the worktree.

findings.title: Branch sprawl: 16 unmerged remote branches, several stale (~3 months)

locations.snippet: git branch -r --no-merged main (16); story/* and jarvis/p* branches stale since 2026-03-24 / 2026-06-13

findings.evidence: Squash-merge workflow leaves source branches behind; for-each-ref shows multiple story/* and jarvis/p* branches with no commits past March/June and no corresponding open PR activity.
findings.impact: Cosmetic clutter and ambiguity about which branches are live work vs. abandoned. No correctness risk, but stale branches accrete confusion and can mask truly-in-flight work.

findings.title: Branch-protection / required-status-checks on `main` not verifiable from worktree
findings.evidence: A well-formed aggregating gate exists (ci-gate needs all per-package jobs and fails on any failure/cancellation), and arch/audit jobs carry comments asserting they cannot be bypassed with --no-verify because they run in CI. But whether GitHub requires ci-gate (and review) before merge to main is a repo-settings fact not present in the checked-out tree.
findings.impact: If the gate is configured-but-not-required, a direct push or an un-gated merge could land unverified code on main (which auto-deploys). The presence of the gate strongly suggests intent to enforce; confirm enforcement.

findings.title: Change failure rate and MTTR not measurable from git alone
findings.evidence: No revert/rollback/hotfix commits in the last 90 days (grep → 0 on first-parent main). Many fix(ci): commits exist but those are forward-fixes during the OIDC-publishing migration, not production incident recoveries. There is no incident-tracking artifact in-repo.
findings.impact: Two of the four DORA metrics are unobservable here. The zero-revert signal is mildly encouraging but not a substitute for measured CFR/MTTR. Collect these from deploy/incident tooling.

findings.title: Single-maintainer bus factor (delivery continuity risk)
findings.evidence: Inventory notes the primary contributor is Alex Jarvis (plus dependabot, Claude, github-actions bots). 91 commits in 90 days are near-entirely one human author.
findings.impact: Delivery automation is excellent and would survive, but release decisions, secret rotation (OIDC/EXPO_TOKEN/Render), and incident response concentrate on one person. Not a CI/CD defect — a continuity note for the delivery system as a whole.

metrics.label: Lead time proxy
metrics.notes: Last ~30 merged PRs: most created→merged same day. From gh pr list

metrics.label: Merge/commit cadence
metrics.value: 15 PRs merged in 90d; 76 first-parent commits to main in 90d

recommendations.title: Confirm (and, if absent, enable) GitHub branch protection on `main` requiring `ci-gate` and a review
recommendations.description: The workflows assume gated merges but enforcement is not in-tree.

recommendations.title: Begin collecting change-failure-rate and MTTR from deploy/incident tooling
recommendations.description: Render deploy history, Netlify/EAS deploy logs, an incident log, so the two unmeasured DORA metrics become observable rather than inferred.

recommendations.title: Prune stale remote branches
recommendations.description: Delete-on-merge for squashed PRs; sweep the story/* and jarvis/p* branches that have no recent activity or open PR.

recommendations.title: Document the secret-rotation and release-recovery runbook for the single-maintainer scenario
recommendations.description: OIDC publisher config, EXPO_TOKEN, Render/Netlify credentials, CODECOV_TOKEN, to reduce bus-factor risk on the delivery path.

dimensions.confidence: Deployment-frequency and lead-time buckets are LLM-estimated from git history and gh pr list metadata, not from observed production deploy events — directionally robust but read as DORA buckets, not precise figures. Deploy-on-push behavior is read from workflow triggers and PaaS config. Change failure rate and MTTR are explicitly not measured (out of reach with git alone). Branch-protection enforcement (F2) is a repo-settings fact not present in the tree. CI/CD config-quality observations are directly observed from reading the seven workflow files in full.

dimensions.title: Documentation & Onboarding
dimensions.summary: The randsum monorepo carries an exceptionally heavy and well-maintained documentation investment that puts it in the top decile for a project of this size. The root README.md hits every checklist item; a complete contributor on-ramp exists via CONTRIBUTING.md plus a root and per-package CLAUDE.md system; there are 19 well-indexed MADR-style ADRs; a real operational runbook (apps/DEPLOY.md) with rollback/DR/token-rotation procedures and an RCA template; two dedicated documentation sites; TypeDoc wired for API generation; and machine-readable docs surfaces (/docs subpath, llms.txt). Onboarding friction is genuinely low — a new contributor can go from clone to green build via a single documented bun run check:all. Gaps are minor and hygienic: TypeDoc output is configured but not published, two READMEs are thin, one known stale README is flagged but not yet fixed.

findings.title: Generated API reference (TypeDoc) is configured but never published
findings.evidence: TypeDoc is fully wired — entry points cover packages/roller/src/index.ts plus the 7 generated game modules, output to docs/api/, private/internal symbols excluded. But docs/api/ is git-ignored and the root README states "publishing the generated output to randsum.dev is out of scope here." The hand-authored */api-reference.mdx pages are the de-facto public API reference and must be kept in sync by hand.
findings.impact: The authoritative, always-current generated API surface is invisible to consumers. Hand-maintained API-reference pages are a doc-rot vector — they can silently drift from the actual exported signatures since nothing regenerates or diffs them against TypeDoc.

findings.title: Known-stale `apps/discord-bot/README.md` flagged but not corrected
findings.evidence: The README still documents a self-hosted run path (node dist/index.js # or via pm2 / systemd / docker), but the committed render.yaml hosts the bot as a Render worker. apps/DEPLOY.md already notes "treat Render as the source of truth for hosting," so the discrepancy is acknowledged in one doc while the other remains uncorrected.
findings.impact: "Doc rot beats no docs" — a confidently-wrong hosting path can send an operator down a pm2/systemd route that does not match production. Low blast radius (private app, single maintainer) keeps this at Medium rather than High.

findings.title: Two thin READMEs: `apps/cli` and `apps/rdn`
findings.evidence: apps/cli is the thinnest README+CLAUDE pair — adequate install/command reference but no architecture or test-pattern notes. apps/rdn/README.md is bare-minimum (26 lines) and delegates almost everything to its CLAUDE.md. No file is below 20 lines, so there are no empty stubs.
findings.impact: Minor onboarding friction for these two surfaces only; both are buffered by solid CLAUDE.md files, so a contributor is not blocked, just under-served relative to the monorepo's high baseline.

findings.title: Runbook coverage is template-only for incidents
findings.evidence: A high-quality RCA template exists (timeline, root-cause vs trigger, detection gap, action items), and apps/DEPLOY.md documents the copy-to-incidents/ workflow, but the incidents/ directory contains only a .gitkeep. This is expected for a project with no recorded outages, not a defect.
findings.impact: None today. The process is documented and ready; there is simply no incident history to learn from. Flagged so it is not mistaken for missing runbook coverage.

findings.title: External dependencies the audit cannot verify (Discord, project board, password vault)

locations.snippet: registrar/Netlify DNS + store credentials in the team password vault

findings.evidence: Roadmap/community routing points to a GitHub project board and Discord; DR notes correctly externalize DNS records and store credentials to a password vault. These are sound practices but live outside the repo and cannot be confirmed by this read-only audit.
findings.impact: Negligible — flagged per the skill's "linked external resources count, but note the gap" guidance. Bus-factor risk concentrates on the single maintainer who holds vault access.

metrics.label: README completeness (root)
metrics.notes: Purpose, quickstart, architecture, contributing link, license — all present plus badges

metrics.label: Runbook coverage
metrics.value: Comprehensive (deploy + rollback + DR + token rotation); incident log empty

metrics.label: Documentation freshness
metrics.value: High — 32 doc-touching commits in last 90 days

metrics.label: Dedicated doc sites
metrics.value: 2 (randsum.dev: 29 MDX pages; notation.randsum.dev: 1,537-line formal spec + 48 conformance vectors)

recommendations.title: Publish the TypeDoc output (or generate + diff it in CI against the hand-authored pages)
recommendations.description: Publish git-ignored docs/api/ to a stable URL and link from README, OR generate in CI and diff against */api-reference.mdx to catch signature drift. Closes the only structural doc-rot vector (F1).

recommendations.title: Correct `apps/discord-bot/README.md` to make Render the documented hosting path
recommendations.description: Or explicitly mark the pm2/systemd/docker block as "alternative self-host, not production," resolving the contradiction already noted in apps/DEPLOY.md (F2).

recommendations.title: Expand the two thin surfaces (`apps/cli`, `apps/rdn`)
recommendations.description: Add architecture/test-pattern notes to apps/cli/CLAUDE.md and a brief structure/deploy section to apps/rdn/README.md so they meet the monorepo's own baseline (F3).

recommendations.title: Capture vault/DNS/store-credential ownership and the bus-factor mitigation
recommendations.description: A short access/ownership note (the DR sections reference the vault but not who holds it) (F5).

dimensions.confidence: Directly observable (high confidence): README/CONTRIBUTING/SECURITY/DEPLOY/ADR contents and line counts, ADR count (19) and recency (git-dated 2026-06-21), TypeDoc config and its git-ignored output, the discord-bot pm2-vs-Render contradiction, doc-site page counts. LLM-estimated: the README-completeness score and per-file quality assessments are checklist judgments, not a docs linter. "Onboarding friction: Low" is an inference, not a timed rehearsal. ADR bodies beyond the index were not all read individually. External resources (Discord, project board, password vault) were not inspected.

dimensions.title: Infrastructure & Platform
dimensions.summary: This repo has no IaC, no containers, and no self-managed orchestration — its deploy surface is entirely managed PaaS (Render worker, two Netlify Astro sites, Expo/EAS native+web, npm via OIDC Trusted Publishing). Scoped to managed-platform posture, the configuration is mature and well above the norm: every deploy surface is config-as-code (render.yaml, two netlify.toml, eas.json/app.json), build toolchains are version-pinned, all GitHub Actions are SHA-pinned, no secrets are committed (Render env vars use sync:false; .env* gitignored), npm publishing uses tokenless OIDC, and a dedicated runbooks/DEPLOY.md documents deploy/rollback/DR/token-rotation for every surface. Residual findings are inherent platform tradeoffs (single-instance Discord worker, deliberately pinned), one self-flagged BUN_VERSION drift risk, and the absence of any drift-detection/IaC-scan tooling. Grade A−.

findings.title: Single-instance Discord worker is a single point of failure
findings.evidence: The Render worker is pinned to one instance. The inline comment documents this is mandatory: a Discord gateway connection must be held by a single process — multiple instances double-process events. plan: starter, region: oregon (single region).
findings.impact: If the single worker / Oregon region is down, the bot is offline until Render reschedules it (no hot standby, no multi-AZ). This is an accepted, correct architectural constraint for a singleton gateway consumer, not a defect — but it remains a genuine availability SPOF with no failover.

findings.title: `BUN_VERSION` dashboard-drift risk between blueprint and live service
findings.evidence: The repo self-flags this: the comment states "The live service was on 1.3.8 — if this blueprint is not auto-synced, update the dashboard env var too." Render Blueprints only apply env-var values on sync; a manually-edited dashboard value can silently diverge from the committed 1.3.14.
findings.impact: Build toolchain drift between code-declared and provisioned state — the canonical config-as-code vs. dashboard drift gap. A divergent live Bun version could produce build/runtime behavior that does not match CI. Partially mitigated by the explicit pin + warning; residual risk is that no automated check confirms the dashboard matches the blueprint.

findings.title: No automated drift detection or config-scan tooling
findings.evidence: Findings here are hypothesis-grade — they read declared config, not provisioned state. There is no checkov/tfsec-equivalent for PaaS configs in CI, no Render Blueprint sync-check, and no Netlify/EAS config linting. The bun audit security workflow covers dependency CVEs, not platform config.
findings.impact: The strong declared posture (pinning, sync:false, runbooks) cannot be automatically confirmed against the live platforms. Drift would go undetected until a deploy breaks. Low because the surface is small (3 platforms) and the runbook documents manual verification paths.

findings.title: Expo app version source is `remote`; native builds not store-submitted
findings.evidence: appVersionSource: remote means the build/version number is owned by EAS servers, not the repo. The native deploy workflow builds on manual dispatch only and the Android submit track is internal/draft; iOS has no submit config at all. DEPLOY.md confirms native builds are "not yet store-submitted."
findings.impact: Version provenance for native binaries is not reproducible from git alone (mitigated: autoIncrement: true + remote source is the EAS-recommended pattern). The half-configured submit path means a native release still requires manual console steps — documented, but not codified.

metrics.label: Build-toolchain version pinning
metrics.notes: BUN_VERSION 1.3.14, NODE_VERSION 22/24, EAS CLI ^20.1.0, actions SHA-pinned

recommendations.title: Add a CI guard that the Render dashboard `BUN_VERSION` matches the committed `render.yaml`
recommendations.description: E.g. a scheduled job hitting the Render API, or enforce Blueprint auto-sync, closing the self-flagged drift gap in F2.

recommendations.title: Confirm the hypothesis-grade declarations against live state once
recommendations.description: Verify Render env vars are all sync:false in the dashboard, Netlify build env matches the .toml, and EAS project bindings are current. Record the verification date in runbooks/DEPLOY.md.

recommendations.title: Document the single-instance constraint as an explicit ADR and consider a lightweight external uptime check
recommendations.description: If/when bot availability becomes a concern. The worker has no inbound URL, so monitor via a heartbeat the bot emits.

recommendations.title: Codify the Expo native submit path
recommendations.description: Add an iOS submit.production block; promote Android beyond internal/draft when ready, so a store release is fully config-as-code rather than partly manual.

recommendations.title: Add a Sentry DSN so the single-instance worker has runtime observability
recommendations.description: The errorTracker seam at apps/discord-bot/src/utils/errorTracker.ts is already wired; a DSN gives the outage-prone SPOF runtime error visibility.

dimensions.confidence: Findings are hypothesis-grade, not provisioned-state-confirmed. This dimension reads committed declarations; it does not call the Render/Netlify/EAS/npm APIs. The live platforms may have drifted (F2 is exactly this risk, self-flagged). Scope was correctly narrowed to managed-PaaS posture per the inventory: no IaC, container, or k8s artifact, so the CIS Docker/K8s, IAM/VPC, and encryption-at-rest sweeps are Not Applicable, not failures. All config files were read in full; the no-committed-secrets finding is directly observed. The single-instance SPOF (F1) severity is a judgment call (Low — intrinsic constraint, non-revenue workload).

ontology.source_of_truth: There is no database — this is a stateless library ecosystem, so the domain model lives entirely in TypeScript type definitions and the declarative `.randsum.json` game specs. The two authoritative sources are `packages/roller/src/types/` (+ `src/notation/types.ts`, `src/errors.ts`) for the dice-engine domain, and `packages/games/src/lib/types.ts` plus the `*.randsum.json` spec files for the game-interpretation domain. Where the two disagree, roller types are canonical for dice mechanics and the spec/meta-schema (`packages/games/randsum.json`) is canonical for game definitions. The formal notation grammar is specified externally at https://notation.randsum.dev.

entities.name: Roll (the roll() operation)
entities.purpose: A request to roll one or more dice pools and combine them into a single total. The central verb of the engine; accepts a number, a notation string, an options object, or several combined.
key_attributes: RollArgument (RollOptions | DiceNotation | special-die notation literal | bare number)

entities.name: RollOptions
entities.purpose: The structured form of a single dice pool to roll — sides, quantity, how it combines arithmetically, and which modifiers apply.

entities.name: RollParams
entities.purpose: A fully resolved roll specification — every option materialized to concrete values, ready to execute and describe. Bridges parsed notation and execution.

entities.name: RollRecord
entities.purpose: The complete audit trail of one executed dice pool — what was rolled, every modifier that touched it, and the final total. The unit of roll history.

entities.name: RollerRollResult
entities.purpose: The full return value of roll() — the combined total across all pools, the flat list of die values, and the per-pool records.

entities.name: ModifierDefinition / Modifier
entities.purpose: A transformation applied to a dice pool — drop, reroll, explode, cap, count, arithmetic, etc. The RANDSUM_MODIFIERS registry is the single source of truth for which modifiers exist and the priority order (19 modifiers).
entities.source: registry at packages/roller/src/modifiers/definitions.ts; one file per modifier

entities.name: ModifierLog
entities.purpose: A record of one modifier's effect on a pool — the frequency delta of values added/removed, plus any 1:1 replacements.

entities.name: DiceNotation / Notation
entities.purpose: The string DSL for expressing a roll (NdS plus modifier suffixes). Parsed natively in the roller — there is no separate notation package (merged in per ADR-005).

entities.name: Token
entities.purpose: A lexical unit of a notation string, produced by the tokenize-only path (UI-safe, no roll behavior). Powers notation builders.

entities.name: NotationDoc
entities.purpose: Static, display-only documentation for each dice type and modifier — derived from the schema registries, safe for any bundling context (no engine).

entities.name: RollTraceStep
entities.purpose: One ordered display step when walking a RollRecord's modifier history for visualization. Discriminated union on kind.

entities.name: RandsumError (and subclasses)
entities.purpose: The error hierarchy. All engine and game failures extend a single base, so callers can catch RandsumError broadly or each subtype specifically.
key_attributes: subclasses: NotationParseError, ModifierError, ValidationError, RollError (roller); SchemaError (games)

entities.name: GameRollResult
entities.purpose: A game-interpreted roll outcome — wraps the raw total/rolls with a typed, game-specific result (e.g. 'critical' | 'success' | 'partial' | 'failure') and optional structured details.

entities.name: RandSumSpec (.randsum.json)
entities.purpose: The declarative definition of a single game's dice mechanics. Code-generated into a typed roll() per game. Defines a four-stage pipeline: Dice → Modify → Resolve → Outcome.

entities.name: RollDefinition
entities.purpose: One named roll within a spec — its inputs, dice pools, modifier chain, resolution strategy, outcome table, and conditional overrides.

entities.name: PoolDefinition
entities.purpose: A named dice pool in a spec — sides (or custom faces) and quantity, either of which may bind to a user input.

entities.name: TableDefinition / TableRange / Outcome
entities.purpose: Outcome resolution — maps a resolved roll value (or pool condition) to a labeled result. The bridge from raw number to game-meaning.

entities.name: InputDeclaration
entities.purpose: A typed, validated user input to a game roll (e.g. Blades' rating, a D&D ability modifier).

entities.name: NormalizedSpec (codegen IR)
entities.purpose: The intermediate representation the codegen pipeline produces by resolving every $ref/$input in a RandSumSpec into a flat, concrete form before emitting TypeScript. Internal, not a published type.
key_attributes: rolls keyed by name, each a NormalizedRollDefinition with ref-free pools, resolve, outcome, and kind-discriminated details fields ('leaf' | 'nested' | 'conditional')

relationships.evidence: root CLAUDE.md; games depend only on roller

glossary.definition: The string DSL for a roll: NdS plus modifier suffixes (4d6L, 2d6+3[fire]). Parsed natively by the roller.
A group of like dice rolled together (quantity × sides).
A transformation on a pool's results (drop/reroll/explode/etc.); 19 exist, run in fixed priority order.
Remove (drop) or retain (keep) the lowest/highest dice before totaling.
Reroll dice matching a condition, up to an optional max.
On a triggering value, roll an extra die and add it (single pass). Variants: compound, penetrate, explodeSequence.
Force all kept dice to distinct values, except listed notUnique.
Clamp rolled values into a comparison range.
Count dice matching a condition instead of summing; deduct subtracts below-threshold from above.
D6-System wild die: compounds on max, removes wild + highest on a 1.
Equivalent to 1d100.
Faces [-1,0,1] (standard) or [-2..2] (extended); sum as signed values.
A dN with faces 0..N-1 instead of 1..N.
Die with explicit numeric or string faces; string faces form a 'table die'.
Sampling without replacement — no repeats until the pool is exhausted.
Roll a dN until the first 1; result is the attempt count.
A free-text tag attached to a roll, carried through to results.
A success test on a pool ('at least 2 dice show 6'), used in outcome tables.
A range/exact/condition lookup mapping a resolved value to a labeled result.
Outcome resolution by thresholds: critical-success/success/failure/critical-failure.
How a pool collapses to a value: sum, faces, countMatching, tableLookup, comparePool*, remoteTableLookup.
A resolve strategy that fetches an external table at codegen time and bakes it in (e.g. Salvage Union).
A game's stable identifier and subpath name (blades, fifth, …).
The pipeline that resolves a .randsum.json spec to a NormalizedSpec and emits a typed *.generated.ts.
Spec-internal pointers to a shared section (#/tables/x) or a declared user input.
The conventional polyhedral set: d4/d6/d8/d10/d12/d20/d100.

contexts.name: Roller Engine (packages/roller)
contexts.ui_surface: Zero-dependency dice core: notation parsing/validation/tokenization, the 19-modifier pipeline, RNG, roll execution, static docs, and trace visualization. Owns all dice mechanics; knows nothing about games.

contexts.name: Game Interpretation (packages/games)
contexts.ui_surface: Wraps the roller with game-specific meaning. Declarative .randsum.json specs are code-generated into typed roll()s. Depends only on the roller; games never depend on each other. Games: blades, daggerheart, fate, fifth, pbta, root-rpg, salvageunion.

contexts.name: Notation Specification (apps/rdn → notation.randsum.dev)
contexts.ui_surface: The formal, external specification of the notation grammar — taxonomy, faceted classification, conformance levels, and the execution-pipeline contract. The authoritative reference the engine conforms to (pre-push runs an @randsum/rdn conformance:check). Documentation context, not runtime entities.

contexts.name: Consumer Apps (apps/cli, apps/discord-bot, apps/site, apps/expo, packages/dice-ui)
contexts.ui_surface: Surfaces that consume the engine and games: the published CLI, the Discord bot (Render worker), the docs site (randsum.dev), the Expo dice playground, and the dice-ui React Native component library. These introduce presentation-layer types but no new domain entities.

ontology.confidence: Directly observable: all roller entities and relationships are read straight from exported TypeScript interfaces with explicit return types (isolatedDeclarations forces this), so they are high-confidence. The error hierarchy, modifier registry, and the four bounded contexts map 1:1 to package boundaries. Spec/game entities are high-confidence from packages/games/src/lib/types.ts and the meta-schema, cross-checked against a real spec (blades.randsum.json). Spec-internal relationships are implicit string pointers ($ref/$input) resolved at codegen time, not type-level links. Correction to the audit brief: the referenced CollectedResults discriminated union with kind: 'union' | 'numeric' | 'opaque' | 'result-mapping' does NOT exist in the codebase; the real result type is RollerRollResult/RollResult. No persistence layer exists — the ontology is derived entirely from types and specs.

items.title: Correct the stale `apps/discord-bot/README.md` hosting path (pm2 → Render)
items.description: Confidently-wrong doc, already acknowledged-but-uncorrected in apps/DEPLOY.md. A one-file fix closing a known doc-rot vector — the highest risk-reduction-per-edit item in the audit.
items.acceptance_criteria: apps/discord-bot/README.md:182 no longer presents `node dist/index.js` via pm2/systemd/docker as the production run path; Render (per render.yaml) is documented as the source of truth; any self-host block is explicitly marked "alternative self-host, not production"; no remaining contradiction with apps/DEPLOY.md:117-118.

items.title: Confirm (and enable if absent) branch protection requiring `ci-gate` on `main`
items.description: `main` auto-deploys to four targets. A configured-but-not-required ci-gate means an un-gated merge could ship unverified code. This only verifies the repo-settings fact the worktree can't see.
items.acceptance_criteria: GitHub branch protection on main requires the ci-gate status check; at least one review/approval is required before merge to main; verification recorded (e.g. a screenshot or a note in a runbook).

items.title: Confirm hypothesis-grade PaaS declarations against live platform state (once)
items.description: The entire infrastructure A-grade rests on declared config never checked against provisioned state. A one-time confirmation converts a hypothesis into a fact cheaply, and seeds the drift gap in N7/L7.
items.acceptance_criteria: Render env vars confirmed all sync:false in the dashboard; live BUN_VERSION matches committed render.yaml (1.3.14); Netlify build env confirmed to match each apps/*/netlify.toml; EAS project bindings confirmed current; verification date recorded in runbooks/DEPLOY.md.

items.title: Add `fast-check` property tests around `parseArguments.ts` multi-pool splitting
items.description: parseArguments.ts is the highest-churn + moderate-complexity file in the engine and the origin of notation edge-case bugs ("1d20+2d6"). Locking its behavior with property tests hardens the core's most bug-prone surface without restructuring it.
items.acceptance_criteria: New *.property.test.ts covers signed/unsigned multi-pool boundaries and special-die collisions (D{..}/DD/d%); tests pass and run in the roller CI job.

items.title: Manifest hygiene: prune unused deps and fix the expo `tsconfig.base` reference
items.description: knip already flags the items; a clean sweep restores full dead-code coverage in CI (the expo tsconfig.base load error currently leaves expo's dead-export sweep partial).
items.acceptance_criteria: The 6 unused (dev)dependencies flagged by bun run knip are removed or justified; apps/expo tsconfig no longer references the missing expo/tsconfig.base; knip completes the expo sweep without load errors.

items.title: Prune stale remote branches (delete-on-merge + sweep story/*, jarvis/p*)
items.description: 16 unmerged remote branches (~7 stale) are cosmetic clutter that masks in-flight work; pruning is trivial and reduces ambiguity.
items.acceptance_criteria: Enable delete-on-merge for squash-merged PRs; stale story/* and jarvis/p* branches with no open PR / recent activity are deleted.

items.title: Activate the Discord-bot error tracker and add a Sentry DSN
items.description: The forwardToSentry body is an empty no-op stub, so even with a DSN set, exceptions never leave Render logs — "observability theater." Merged with infrastructure R5 (same seam, same fix): the single-instance worker's outage-prone SPOF currently has no runtime error aggregation.
items.acceptance_criteria: forwardToSentry (apps/discord-bot/src/utils/errorTracker.ts:42) delivers to a real tracker (Sentry SDK or a dependency-thin HTTP-DSN POST), gated on SENTRY_DSN; a Sentry DSN (or equivalent) is configured for the deployed worker; a test exercises the forward path (mocked transport) so the stub can't silently regress.

items.title: Add an application-level liveness signal + gateway instrumentation for the bot
items.description: The bot is a Render worker with no HTTP health check; a "zombie" state (process alive, gateway silently disconnected) is invisible until a user reports it. The numInstances:1 SPOF (correct and intrinsic) has no failover, so detection is the only available mitigation.
items.acceptance_criteria: discord.js shardDisconnect/shardReconnecting/shardError events are instrumented into the logger + metrics; a lightweight watchdog or /healthz-style heartbeat makes a stuck-but-alive gateway externally detectable (an emitted heartbeat the worker pushes, since it has no inbound URL).

items.title: Define one symptom-based alert and a minimal SLO/SLI for the bot
items.description: Per-command metrics already flush every 5 min, but nothing closes the loop — the signal is forensic-only. One alert + a one-line SLO turns existing metrics into incident detection.
items.acceptance_criteria: One symptom-based alert is defined and routed to a notification channel (e.g. command error-rate over a 5-min window, or absence of bot.ready / metrics.flush lines); a minimal SLO/SLI + error-budget one-liner is recorded.

items.title: Add unit tests for the load-bearing bot reliability utilities
items.description: loginWithBackoff, metrics, logger, and the interactionCreate error-reply fallback are the components that only run under failure — exactly the untested code that ships regressions silently. loginWithBackoff's injectable sleep seam signals test intent that was never realized.
items.acceptance_criteria: Unit tests cover loginWithBackoff (exhaustion, cap, success-on-retry via the injectable sleep), metrics (flush/snapshot/timer lifecycle), logger (Error serialization, level→stream routing), and the interactionCreate error-reply fallback (replied/deferred branches); tests run in the discord-bot CI job.

items.title: Publish the generated TypeDoc API reference (or diff it against hand-authored pages in CI)
items.description: TypeDoc is fully wired but docs/api/ is git-ignored and unpublished; the hand-authored */api-reference.mdx pages are the de-facto public reference and can silently drift from exported signatures. This is the only structural doc-rot vector in an otherwise A-grade doc estate.
items.acceptance_criteria: Either: TypeDoc output is published to a stable URL and linked from the README; Or: CI generates TypeDoc and diffs it against the */api-reference.mdx pages, failing on signature drift.

items.title: Broaden app-layer integration/e2e coverage (replicate the discord-bot pattern)
items.description: The discord-bot's un-mocked cross-boundary integration test is exemplary but unique; the cli app has no equivalent and the docs-site builds have no smoke e2e. This is the only marked gap holding test-quality below a full A.
items.acceptance_criteria: An un-mocked cross-boundary integration test exists for the cli app (mirroring the discord-bot pattern: real engine + seeded random → asserted output); at least a smoke e2e exists for the site / rdn docs builds.

items.title: Add a CI guard that live Render `BUN_VERSION` matches the committed blueprint
items.description: The repo self-flags this drift risk (the live service was on 1.3.8 vs committed 1.3.14). A divergent live Bun version could produce build/runtime behavior that doesn't match CI. After the one-time N3 confirmation, this makes the check standing.
items.acceptance_criteria: A CI/scheduled job (Render API) asserts the live BUN_VERSION matches render.yaml, OR Blueprint auto-sync is enforced so divergence is impossible.

items.title: Extract shared logic from the `dice-ui` web/native component pairs
items.description: The QuickReferenceGrid.tsx (983 LOC) / .native.tsx (792 LOC) pair has ~1,459 differing lines — effectively two parallel components, the repo's top duplication/modifiability risk. Placed in Later because it is contained to a single private package and is a substantial refactor, not a quick fix.
items.acceptance_criteria: Shared logic for the diverged dice-ui pairs (starting with QuickReferenceGrid) is extracted into platform-agnostic hooks/utilities; each .tsx/.native.tsx is reduced to a thin render shim over the shared logic.

items.title: Decompose the oversized codegen emitter `generateFunctionBody`
items.description: generateFunctionBody (~157 lines) is the single longest function in the tree and is string-emitting codegen — bugs propagate to all eight game packages at once. Decomposing it lowers the blast radius of codegen changes.
items.acceptance_criteria: generateFunctionBody (and siblings generateMultiPoolBody, generateRollParts in emitBody.ts) are split into smaller named sub-emitters by concern (pool / modifier / multi-pool join); gen:check still passes.

items.title: Add a commit-time secret-scanning gate
items.description: The repo is clean today, but no dedicated scanner (gitleaks / detect-secrets / GitHub push protection) is wired into lefthook or CI. Pure prevention against future accidental commits — cheap.
items.acceptance_criteria: gitleaks or detect-secrets runs in lefthook.yml pre-commit, and/or GitHub push protection is enabled; a baseline pass is clean.

items.title: Document the RNG security boundary (`Math.random` is non-crypto)
items.description: The product's value proposition is dice fairness, yet the default RNG is Math.random (non-cryptographic). Documenting the boundary + the { randomFn } injection pattern prevents misuse in any future money/wager / commit-reveal context.
items.acceptance_criteria: @randsum/roller docs/README state that the default RNG is entertainment-grade and non-cryptographic; the { randomFn } injection pattern (e.g. backed by crypto.getRandomValues) is shown for CSPRNG-grade consumers.

items.title: Begin collecting change-failure-rate and MTTR from deploy/incident tooling
items.description: Two of the four DORA metrics are unobservable from git. Sourcing them from Render/Netlify/EAS deploy history (and any incident log) completes the delivery picture. Strategic, not urgent — delivery already grades A.
items.acceptance_criteria: CFR and MTTR are derived from deploy/incident tooling and recorded somewhere durable (dashboard or periodic note), rather than inferred from zero-revert git history.

items.title: Note the bench gate's `roller`-only trigger scope (gate exists; verify breadth)
items.description: Reconciliation note — performance R1 claims no bench gate exists; this is stale. The gate does exist (bench (regression gate), fail-on-alert: true, threshold 150%, baseline cached). The only residual nuance: the job is gated on needs.changes.outputs.roller == 'true', so a games/CLI-only change won't re-run or re-baseline the benchmark.
items.acceptance_criteria: A maintainer confirms the roller-path-only trigger is intentional, OR the bench job's path filter is broadened if non-roller code can affect roll-engine latency; performance R1's "no gate exists" claim is corrected in any downstream tracking.

items.title: Watch the two tight roller bundle budgets (`dist/index.js`, `dist/tokenize.js`)
items.description: dist/index.js (1.7% headroom) and dist/tokenize.js (3.7% headroom) will trip size-limit on the next non-trivial barrel addition or a behavior leak into the tokenize import graph. The gate already catches it — this is "be aware in review," not a fix.
items.acceptance_criteria: The dist/tokenize.js isolation invariant (schema must not reference behavior symbols at module-init) is documented as a review checkpoint; any new modifier/notation primitive triggers a deliberate budget-vs-trim decision rather than an accidental CI failure.

items.title: Guard the dist-smoke tests against a missing `dist/`
items.description: A fresh-worktree bun test without a prior build surfaces 15+ confusing Cannot-find-module dist/index.js failures that are really a missing build step (CI never hits this — pre-push runs build at priority 1). A skip-with-message guard makes the failure mode self-explanatory to new contributors.
items.acceptance_criteria: The (dist) smoke tests detect a missing dist/ and test.skip with a "run bun run build first" message instead of erroring opaquely.

items.title: Capture vault/DNS/credential ownership and a secret-rotation/release-recovery runbook (bus-factor)
items.description: Single-maintainer continuity risk recurs across three dimensions: release decisions, secret rotation (OIDC / EXPO_TOKEN / Render / Netlify / CODECOV_TOKEN), DNS, vault access, and incident response all concentrate on one person. Automation would survive a bus event; the human-held knowledge would not.
items.acceptance_criteria: A runbook documents secret-rotation steps for OIDC publisher config, EXPO_TOKEN, Render/Netlify credentials, and CODECOV_TOKEN; vault / DNS / store-credential ownership (who holds access) is recorded.

items.title: Expand the two thin READMEs (`apps/cli`, `apps/rdn`)
items.description: Both surfaces are below the monorepo's high doc baseline (buffered by solid CLAUDE.md files, so no contributor is blocked — just under-served).
items.acceptance_criteria: apps/cli/CLAUDE.md gains architecture / test-pattern notes; apps/rdn/README.md gains a brief structure/deploy section.

items.title: Keep coupling-magnet `lib/` hubs and the largest test file under periodic review
items.description: roller/src/lib (~12 fan-in) and games/src/lib (~9) are intentional, contained hubs today; the only risk is lib/utils accreting unrelated helpers over time. Likewise notation/definitions.test.ts (987 LOC) is fine as a data table but should split by category if it grows. Watch items, not remediation.
items.acceptance_criteria: A periodic-review note exists for roller/src/lib/utils (split by concern if it accretes unrelated helpers); notation/definitions.test.ts is split by notation category if it grows materially beyond its current size.

items.title: Make the `apps/cli` → roller dependency edge explicit
items.description: apps/cli imports @randsum/roller/roll but lists roller under devDependencies (defensible — bunup inlines it at build), making the edge invisible to manifest-reading tooling. Manifest-hygiene nuance only.
items.acceptance_criteria: Either roller is moved to dependencies in apps/cli/package.json, OR a comment documents the bundled-edge rationale; the published CLI bundle is confirmed self-contained.

items.title: Re-evaluate suppressed dev/build-time advisories on the next Astro/Expo major bump
items.description: The 4 moderate + 1 low advisories suppressed in osv-scanner.toml are correctly dev/build-time-only and unreachable from published packages today, but suppressions should expire, not become permanent. Optionally pair with a full-tree SCA/license scanner as a v2 confirmation.
items.acceptance_criteria: The osv-scanner.toml suppressions are re-checked at the next Astro/Expo major bump and removed if resolved; (optional) a full-transitive-tree SCA + license scan is run once to confirm the manifest-level findings.

items.title: Codify the Expo native store-submit path; consider bot rate-limiting
items.description: Two small defense-in-depth / completeness items: the Expo native submit path is half-configured (no iOS submit.production; Android internal/draft), so a native release still requires manual console steps; and the single-instance bot worker caps a single roll's work but not a flood of large-but-legal rolls.
items.acceptance_criteria: An iOS submit.production block is added and the Android track promoted beyond internal/draft when a store release is intended; (optional) a basic per-user/-guild rate limit guards the bot's compute commands.

items.title: Capture the missing-config failure path through `captureException` (minor consistency)
items.description: config.ts throws at import time before initErrorTracker() runs, so a missing-env failure lands only as a raw stderr stack while the auth-failure path is hardened — a minor asymmetry. Optionally also add happy-path interaction-level correlation ids (F5) for easier log reconstruction.
items.acceptance_criteria: initErrorTracker() runs before (or the config.ts throw is restructured so) all boot failures route through captureException.

cross_cutting_themes: Bot observability "last mile" — Reliability F1–F4 and Infrastructure F1/R5 all collapse into one effort: activate the tracker (N7), add liveness/alerting (N8, N9), and test the failure-path utilities (N10). Fixing this once lifts the bot's reliability posture from B to A.
Single-maintainer bus factor — recurs across Delivery, Documentation, and Infrastructure; one ownership + secret-rotation runbook (L9) addresses all three.
Doc-vs-reality drift — the stale bot README (N1) and unpublished TypeDoc (N11) are the only structural doc-rot vectors in an otherwise top-decile doc estate.
"Configured but unverified" platform posture — branch protection (N2), PaaS-vs-live state (N3), and BUN_VERSION drift (N13) all convert declared config into confirmed state.
remediation.confidence: Most items rest on directly observable evidence (file reads, knip, bun audit, dependency-cruiser, size-limit, grep sweeps). LLM judgment was used for severity/risk-reduction ranking and for maintainability complexity buckets (function lengths are ±10% heuristics). One source-level discrepancy was reconciled against ci.yml: the performance report's claim that "no benchmark regression gate exists" is incorrect — the gate exists (bench (regression gate), fail-on-alert at 150%), matching the delivery report; that recommendation is dropped as already-satisfied and only its residual nuance (roller-path-only trigger) is carried (L6). Effort, duration, and team-shape estimates are deliberately out of scope; the Now/Next/Later buckets are priority groupings, not time buckets.
